questions.question: I _____ to Paris three times in my life.
questions.explanation: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) is used for life experiences when we don't say WHEN. 'Three times in my life' is a clear signal for present perfect.

questions.question: She has lived in this town _____ 2020.
questions.explanation: Use 'since' with a point in time (since 2020, since Monday). Use 'for' with a length of time (for 5 years, for two hours).

questions.question: When I got home, I realised I _____ my keys at school. (forget)
questions.explanation: Past perfect (had + past participle) shows an action that happened BEFORE another action in the past. I forgot the keys → then I realised.

questions.question: If I _____ a million pounds, I would buy a big house with a swimming pool.
questions.explanation: Second conditional describes an imaginary or unlikely situation: if + past simple, ... would + base verb.

questions.question: When I was younger, I _____ ride my bike to school every day. Now I take the bus.
questions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' describes past habits or states that are no longer true today.

questions.question: Tom said, "I am hungry." → Tom said that he _____ hungry.
questions.explanation: In reported speech, present simple usually changes to past simple: am → was, is → was, are → were.

questions.question: The cake _____ by my grandma yesterday. (bake)
questions.explanation: Past simple passive: was/were + past participle. The subject (the cake) receives the action — someone else did it.

questions.question: John is wearing a thick coat and a scarf. It _____ be cold outside.
questions.explanation: 'Must be' shows we are quite sure something is true, based on the evidence we can see.

questions.question: That's the boy _____ won the running race yesterday.
questions.explanation: 'Who' is for people. 'Which' is for things or animals. 'Where' is for places.

questions.question: Please _____ your wet shoes before you come into the house.
questions.explanation: 'Take off' = remove clothes, shoes, hats. The opposite is 'put on'.

questions.question: She is a really _____ person — she always finishes her work properly. (CARE)
questions.explanation: Add -ful to certain nouns to make adjectives meaning 'full of': care → careful, help → helpful, use → useful.

questions.question: There isn't _____ milk in the fridge. We need to buy some.
questions.explanation: Use 'much' with uncountable nouns (milk, water, sugar). Use 'many' with countable nouns (apples, books).

questions.question: _____ it was raining heavily, we decided to go to the park.
questions.explanation: 'Although' joins two contrasting ideas in one sentence. 'However' usually starts a new sentence with a comma after it.

questions.question: Make a question:
questions.explanation: Present perfect questions: question word (How many) + noun + auxiliary (have) + subject + past participle + ?
questions.correctSentence: How many books have you read ?

questions.question: I wish I _____ a dog, but my parents say no.
questions.explanation: After 'I wish' for present situations we want to be different, we use the past simple: I wish I had...

questions.question: I want _____ a doctor when I grow up because I want to help people.
questions.explanation: After 'want', we use 'to + base verb' (the to-infinitive): want to be, want to go, want to learn.

questions.question: Put the words in order:
questions.explanation: Adverbs of frequency (always, never, often, sometimes) usually go BEFORE the main verb: They always have...

questions.question: You _____ told me about the meeting earlier! Now we're going to be late.
questions.explanation: 'Should have + past participle' = a good idea in the past that didn't happen. (You didn't tell me — but it would have been a good idea.)

questions.question: I always _____ my homework right after dinner.
questions.explanation: We say 'do homework', 'do exercise', 'do the washing'. We MAKE food, beds, and mistakes.

questions.question: She enjoys _____ in the school choir every Friday.
questions.explanation: After 'enjoy', 'finish', 'avoid' and 'mind', we use the -ing form: enjoy singing, enjoy reading, enjoy travelling.

questions.question: Why is Maya excited?
questions.explanation: The text says: 'next week she's going to start at a new school.'
questions.reading: Maya is excited because next week she's going to start at a new school. She has been living in a new town with her family for two months. At first she felt a bit nervous, but she has already made some good friends in her neighbourhood. Maya's biggest passion is photography, so she's hoping to join the school's photography club, where she can take pictures and share them with other students who love art too.
options: She has won a photography prize
She is going to start at a new school
She is moving to a new town

questions.question: How long has Maya been living in the new town?
questions.explanation: The text says: 'She has been living in a new town with her family for two months.'
questions.reading: Maya is excited because next week she's going to start at a new school. She has been living in a new town with her family for two months. At first she felt a bit nervous, but she has already made some good friends in her neighbourhood. Maya's biggest passion is photography, so she's hoping to join the school's photography club, where she can take pictures and share them with other students who love art too.

questions.question: What does Maya hope to do at her new school?
questions.explanation: The text says she is hoping to 'join the school's photography club'.
questions.reading: Maya is excited because next week she's going to start at a new school. She has been living in a new town with her family for two months. At first she felt a bit nervous, but she has already made some good friends in her neighbourhood. Maya's biggest passion is photography, so she's hoping to join the school's photography club, where she can take pictures and share them with other students who love art too.

questions.question: Today's weather is _____ than yesterday's. The sun is shining!
questions.explanation: 'Good' is irregular: good → better → best. We never say 'gooder' or 'more good'.

questions.question: Mount Everest is _____ mountain in the world.
questions.explanation: For short adjectives in the superlative, add -est and use 'the': the highest, the tallest, the longest.

questions.question: She didn't go to school yesterday, _____?
questions.explanation: Negative statement → positive tag question. 'didn't go' → 'did she?'

questions.question: I'll text you _____ I get home.
questions.explanation: 'As soon as' = the moment that something happens. (Note: after 'as soon as', we use the present simple, not 'will'.)

questions.question: _____ being tired, she finished all her homework.
questions.explanation: 'Despite' is followed by a noun or -ing form (despite being tired). 'Although' is followed by a full clause (although she was tired).

questions.question: We should _____ paper and plastic to help protect the planet.
questions.explanation: 'Recycle' = use materials again to make new things, instead of throwing them away as rubbish.

questions.question: I'm trying to _____ a few words of Italian before our holiday.
questions.explanation: 'Pick up a language' = learn it gradually, often without much formal study, just by listening and using it.

questions.question: Make a present perfect question:
questions.explanation: Present perfect questions: Have/Has + subject + (ever) + past participle + ...? 'Ever' goes before the main verb.
questions.correctSentence: Have you ever been to Japan ?

questions.question: Please don't _____ so much noise — your sister is studying!
questions.explanation: We MAKE noise, mistakes, decisions, plans, friends. We DO homework, exercise, the washing-up.

questions.question: She _____ her hair cut at the salon last weekend.
questions.explanation: 'Have something done' means someone else does it for you. She didn't cut her own hair — the hairdresser did it.

questions.question: If you _____ hard, you will pass the exam easily. (study)
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple, ... will + base verb. We use it for real possibilities in the future.

questions.question: Maths is sometimes hard. _____, I really enjoy it.
questions.explanation: 'However' shows contrast and usually starts a new sentence with a comma after it: '...hard. However, I enjoy it.'

questions.question: Is there _____ at the door? I think I heard a knock.
questions.explanation: In questions, we usually use 'anyone', 'anything' or 'anywhere'. 'Someone' is more common in positive sentences.

questions.question: Where did Tom and his family go on holiday?
questions.explanation: The text says: 'They travelled to a small island in Greece.'
questions.reading: Last summer, Tom went on an unforgettable holiday with his family. They travelled to a small island in Greece, where they spent two weeks swimming in the clear blue sea and exploring tiny villages. Tom had never tasted Greek food before, but he loved it — especially a dish called moussaka. By the end of the holiday, he had made friends with several children at the campsite, and they promised to write to each other when they got home.

questions.question: How did Tom feel about the Greek food?
questions.explanation: The text says: 'he loved it — especially a dish called moussaka.'
questions.reading: Last summer, Tom went on an unforgettable holiday with his family. They travelled to a small island in Greece, where they spent two weeks swimming in the clear blue sea and exploring tiny villages. Tom had never tasted Greek food before, but he loved it — especially a dish called moussaka. By the end of the holiday, he had made friends with several children at the campsite, and they promised to write to each other when they got home.
options: He had tasted it many times before

questions.question: The film was so _____. I almost fell asleep!
questions.explanation: -ing adjectives describe the THING (the film is boring). -ed adjectives describe HOW YOU FEEL (I am bored).

questions.question: I have to _____ early tomorrow because of the school trip.
questions.explanation: 'Wake up' = stop sleeping. The opposite is 'fall asleep' or 'go to bed'.

questions.question: Write the opposite of 'cheap': _____
questions.explanation: 'Cheap' (low price) and 'expensive' (high price) are opposites.

questions.question: It was _____ a beautiful day that we decided to go for a long walk.
questions.explanation: 'Such + (a/an) + adjective + noun' (such a beautiful day). Use 'so' before just an adjective: so beautiful.

questions.question: He speaks English _____. (perfect)
questions.explanation: To form adverbs from most adjectives, add -ly: perfect → perfectly, slow → slowly, careful → carefully.

questions.question: We need to bring our _____ to enter another country.
questions.explanation: A passport is the official document that proves who you are and lets you travel internationally.

questions.question: _____ you mind opening the window, please? It's so hot in here.
questions.explanation: 'Would you mind + -ing form' is a very polite way to ask someone to do something.

questions.question: She finished _____ her homework before dinner. (do)
questions.explanation: After 'finish', 'enjoy', 'avoid' and 'mind', we use the -ing form: finished doing, finished eating.

questions.question: I felt really _____ when I won first prize. I couldn't stop smiling!
questions.explanation: 'Proud' = feeling very happy and pleased about something good that you (or someone close to you) did.

questions.category: Giving Advice: If I were you
questions.skill: Giving Advice: If I were you
questions.question: If I were you, I _____ talk to the teacher about it.
questions.explanation: 'If I were you, I would + base verb' is a common, friendly way to give advice in English.

questions.question: It was lovely seeing you. I look forward to _____ you again soon.
questions.explanation: 'Look forward to' is followed by a noun or the -ing form: looking forward to seeing, meeting, hearing.

questions.question: Thank you for inviting me. I had a _____ time at your party!
questions.explanation: After a fun party or visit, we usually say we 'had a wonderful (or great / lovely / fantastic) time'.
